metadata.summary: Inject fault modes (timeouts, errors, rate limits, corrupted data, disconnects) at random trajectory points to measure agent resilience and distinguish recovery from confident wrong answers.

content.title: Chaos-Eval — Fault Injection on Agent Tools
content.summary: A standard eval set runs tools that always succeed. Production tools fail: timeouts, 5xx errors, rate limits, corrupted return values, MCP server disconnects. Chaos-eval injects each failure mode at random points along the agent's trajectory and grades not "did it succeed?" but "did it recover, retry intelligently, escalate when blocked, or produce a confidently-wrong answer?". Without it, your reported success rate is a sunny-day distribution that lies the moment the network blinks.

why.p: ReliabilityBench (arXiv 2601.06112, 2026) and the agent-chaos benchmark show that agents which pass standard evals at 90%+ frequently collapse below 50% under realistic fault rates of 5-10%. The 2025 work on stress-testing LLM agents (arXiv 2505.03096) replicates this across six frameworks. Chaos-eval is the only way to measure the resilience axis before users do; it also produces the failure-mode catalogue that drives retry policy and escalation design.

list.item: Any agent past prototype that depends on external tools, web APIs, MCP servers, or remote LLMs.
Pre-launch readiness gate alongside outcome and trajectory evals.
Regression suite when retry / circuit-breaker / fallback logic changes.
Building a "graceful degradation" story for SLAs and post-mortems.
Air-gapped agents with only deterministic local tools (rare in practice).
Pure prompt-only generators with zero tool use — there is nothing to fault-inject.
Pre-prototype phase — wait until tool surface is stable before investing.
Tools whose failure modes are already exercised by record/replay traces from production — chaos-eval is for failure modes you have not yet seen in prod.

section.name: Five canonical failure modes for agent tools
section.p: Inject these five faults at randomized trajectory points; together they cover the failure distribution observed by ReliabilityBench and agent-chaos.

list.item: tool_timeout — call hangs past a configured deadline.
tool_error — call returns a 4xx/5xx or raises an exception.
llm_rate_limit — the LLM provider returns 429 with retry-after.
tool_mutate — call returns plausible but corrupted data (wrong type, missing field, slightly-off number).
service_disconnect — long-lived connection drops mid-stream (MCP server, websocket, streaming LLM).

rule.statement: A chaos-eval middleware must be able to inject each of the five canonical faults at a configurable per-call probability AND at a fixed deterministic step index for reproducibility. Both interfaces are required.
rule.rationale: Probability gives realistic distributions; deterministic step-index gives reproducible regression cases. ReliabilityBench (2026) and agent-chaos both expose both interfaces because random-only injection prevents reliable bug fixing.

example.title: Random and deterministic fault injection
example.code: chaos = ChaosInjector(faults=[ Fault("tool_timeout", p=0.10), Fault("tool_error", p=0.05, code=500), Fault("llm_rate_limit", p=0.02), Fault("tool_mutate", p=0.03), Fault("service_disconnect", p=0.01), ], seed=42) # OR deterministic regression: chaos = ChaosInjector(injections={"step_4": "tool_timeout"})
example.note: Both random and deterministic surfaces — usable for fuzzing and for fixed regression cases.

example.title: Ad-hoc flakiness, not chaos
example.code: if random.random() < 0.1: raise TimeoutError() # ad-hoc, no seed, no taxonomy, no replay
example.note: Single failure mode, no determinism, no scoring — not a chaos suite, just flakiness.

antipattern.description: Injecting only "tool_error" because it is the easiest to simulate.
antipattern.reason: The most damaging real-world failure is tool_mutate (corrupted data) — agents react to wrong-but-plausible returns with confident wrong answers. Skipping mutate guarantees you never measure that risk.

rule.statement: Faults must be injectable at every tool boundary AND at every LLM call boundary, not only at "user-facing" tools. Hidden internal tools (vector lookup, MCP discovery, schema fetch) are the ones that hurt most when they fail silently.
rule.rationale: Most production-fragility comes from invisible infra calls; a chaos suite that only fuzzes the visible surface misses the actual failure distribution.

section.name: Four outcomes: grading recovery
section.p: Under fault injection, "did it succeed?" is the wrong question. Grade each run on a four-outcome rubric so you can tell graceful failure from confident wrongness.

list.item: recovered — agent retried, succeeded within budget, returned a correct answer.
retried_intelligently — agent retried with backoff or alternate tool; outcome may be correct or escalated.
escalated_correctly — agent gave up after bounded retries and surfaced an honest "I cannot complete X because Y".
wrong_confident — agent produced a confident answer that is wrong because it ignored or papered over the fault.

rule.statement: Grade each chaos-eval run on the four outcomes; track per-outcome rates over time and treat any non-zero wrong_confident rate as a launch-blocker.
rule.rationale: Recovered + retried + escalated are all acceptable. Wrong_confident is the failure mode users cannot detect themselves — it silently destroys trust and is easily masked by aggregate "success rate" metrics.

example.title: Four-outcome rubric with launch blocker
example.note: Four buckets, hard gate on wrong_confident.

example.title: Aggregate rate hides outcomes
example.code: success_rate = sum(r.success for r in chaos_runs) / len(chaos_runs) assert success_rate >= 0.85
example.note: Aggregate rate hides confident-wrong outputs inside the "fail" bucket without separating recoverable failures from silent corruption.

antipattern.description: Grading chaos-eval with the same boolean "success" used for sunny-day eval.
antipattern.reason: Sunny-day eval has no faults, so "fail" only means "incorrect". Under fault injection "fail" lumps together honest escalation, retry timeout, and confident wrongness — three completely different reliability stories.

rule.statement: Use chaos-eval results to drive concrete retry-policy parameters: max retries, backoff base, per-fault circuit-breaker thresholds, and escalation triggers. Policy values must be justified by a chaos-eval data point, not by guess.
rule.rationale: Retry policies tuned without chaos data either retry too much (cost explosion) or too little (avoidable failures). The grading rubric directly produces the parameters needed to converge a stable policy.

template.purpose: Reusable fault-injection config: per-fault probability, seed, scope (per-tool or global), recovery thresholds.

see-also.link: ReliabilityBench — fault taxonomy and benchmark methodology.
agent-chaos — open-source chaos middleware for LLM agents.
Stress-testing LLM agents under tool failure (2025).
